home.hero_title: Serving Ranchita by Building Unity, Promoting Resilience, and Empowering Community Voices
home.hero_subtitle: The Montezuma Valley Volunteer Community Service Organization has served Ranchita since 1973.
home.mission_text: MVVCSO exists to strengthen the Ranchita community through emergency preparedness, food security, youth engagement, senior support, and neighbor-to-neighbor volunteerism. We are 100% volunteer-run and have served this backcountry community since 1973.
home.newsletter_subtitle: Get the Ranchita Resilience Report — community news, events, and resources.
home.newsletter_success: You're subscribed! Welcome to the community.
home.donate_cta_text: Every dollar builds a stronger Ranchita. Your donation funds emergency preparedness, food security, youth programs, and more.

about.title: Our Story
about.subtitle: From volunteer firefighters to community champions — over 50 years of service in the backcountry.
about.board_subtitle: Seven dedicated community members guiding MVVCSO into its next chapter.
about.transparency_text: MVVCSO is committed to openness and accountability. Board meeting minutes, financial reports, and organizational documents will be published here as they become available.

programs.title: Our Programs
programs.subtitle: Strengthening Ranchita through community service, emergency preparedness, and neighbor-to-neighbor support.
programs.cert_description: Community Emergency Response Team training to prepare residents for disasters and emergencies.
programs.food_description: Partnering with Feeding San Diego and facilitating CalFresh enrollment for residents in need.
programs.senior_description: Programs and services supporting Ranchita's elderly residents with health, safety, and connection.
programs.youth_description: The Ranchita Youth Council provides leadership, mentorship, and educational opportunities for young residents.
programs.emergency_description: Disaster readiness programs, communication plans, and resilience workshops for the backcountry.
programs.volunteer_description: Coordinating volunteer efforts across all programs. Over 50 years of neighbors helping neighbors.
programs.cta_text: We're always looking for dedicated volunteers to help serve the Ranchita community.

blog.subtitle: News, stories, and resources from the Ranchita community.

pct.title: Virtual PCT Hike
pct.subtitle: Explore the Pacific Crest Trail through Ranchita's backyard — the heart of San Diego's backcountry.
pct.community_text: The Pacific Crest Trail passes through our community. Ranchita has served as a resupply point for thru-hikers for decades, offering water, rest, and warm welcomes to weary travelers.

meta.title: Wildfire Preparedness for Ranchita
meta.description: Backcountry-tailored wildfire preparedness for Ranchita and the Montezuma Valley. Built on CAL FIRE's Ready for Wildfire program and San Diego County's Ready, Set, Go! framework, with Ranchita-specific guidance for defensible space, animals and livestock, evacuation, and mutual aid.

hero.title: Wildfire Preparedness
hero.subtitle: What we do here in the backcountry — built on CAL FIRE and San Diego County guidance, layered with the realities of Ranchita.

why.heading: Why this matters in Ranchita
why.para_1: Ranchita sits at the summit of S22, surrounded by chaparral and pine-oak fuels that ember-drive fires across long distances under Santa Ana wind conditions. The threat is year-round, not just summer and fall, and ember storms can ignite homes well beyond the visible fire line.
why.para_2: Professional firefighting response is volunteer-supported and farther away than in town. That's not a problem to fix — it's the geography we live in. It does mean that the most consequential first response in the first minutes of any fire is almost always residents who are ready, calm, and clear about what to do.

ready_set_go.tip_body: Ready–Set–Go is the official wildfire-action framework used by CAL FIRE and San Diego County Fire. The four sections below extend it with Ranchita-specific context — what defensible space looks like on chaparral parcels, how to plan for multi-animal households, which routes lead out of the valley, and how neighbors support firefighters without becoming obstacles.

prepare.title: Prepare
prepare.description: Defensible space, home hardening, rural-property specifics, and seasonal maintenance.

intro.lead: Defensible space and home hardening together are the most consequential things a Ranchita property owner can do before a fire. They are also the things that no fire engine can do for you on the day of.

zones.heading: Defensible space — the three zones
zones.lead: California's defensible-space framework divides the area around a structure into concentric zones, each with progressively less restrictive maintenance requirements as distance from the home increases. The distances are measured from the exterior walls of the home (or from the edge of attached features like decks). On slopes, recommended distances increase because fire travels faster uphill.
zones.diagram_label_zone1: Zone 1 — 5 to 30 ft (50 ft in San Diego County)
zones.diagram_label_zone2: Zone 2 — 30 to 100 ft (50 to 100 ft in San Diego County)
zones.diagram_aria: Top-down diagram of defensible-space zones around a house: Zone 0 immediately around the structure, Zone 1 extending outward, Zone 2 extending further outward.
zones.sd_county_note: San Diego County requires 50 ft of clearance in Zone 1, stricter than the state minimum.
zones.slope_note: On slopes, extend Zone 1 and Zone 2 distances. Steeper slope = more extension.

zone0.heading: Zone 0 — the ember-resistant zone (0 to 5 ft)
zone0.regulatory_note: Zone 0 was created by Assembly Bill 3074 (2020) and updated by Senate Bill 504 (2024). The state Board of Forestry and Fire Protection completed rulemaking in late 2025 under Governor Newsom's Executive Order N-18-25. Zone 0 is currently a strongly recommended practice with regulatory adoption progressing — apply it now whether or not enforcement has reached your parcel.
zone0.body: Replace bark mulch and combustible landscaping in the immediate 5 ft around the home with hardscape (gravel, pavers, concrete). Remove all dead and dying plants. Clear leaves and needles from roof, gutters, deck, porch, and stairs. Trim branches at least 10 ft from chimneys and stovepipes. Remove combustible furniture, planters, doormats, and stored firewood from within 5 ft of the home. The first 5 ft of any deck attached to the house is part of Zone 0 — keep it clear.

zone1.heading: Zone 1 — lean, clean, and green (5 to 30 ft, or 5 to 50 ft in San Diego County)
zone1.required_action: San Diego County requires 50 ft of clearance in Zone 1, stricter than the state minimum. Plan to 50 ft.
zone1.body: Remove dead vegetation. Trim tree branches at least 6 ft above the ground. Maintain vertical spacing between shrubs and trees — a useful rule of thumb is shrub height multiplied by three for clearance to the lowest tree branch. Maintain horizontal spacing between shrubs and trees, with more space required on steeper slopes and for taller vegetation. Do not stack firewood against the house. Outbuildings and propane tanks need 10 ft of bare mineral soil clearance and an additional 10 ft of no-flammable-vegetation perimeter beyond that.

zone2.heading: Zone 2 — fuel reduction (30 to 100 ft, or 50 to 100 ft in San Diego County)
zone2.body: Mow annual grass to a maximum height of 4 inches. Remove fallen leaves, needles, twigs, bark, cones, and small branches — depth of 3 inches is the maximum acceptable. Thin overgrown shrubs and trees so that crowns do not interlock. Adjust distances upward where slope amplifies fire behavior.

slope.heading: Slope adjustments
slope.body: Fire travels faster uphill. On slopes, extend Zone 1 and Zone 2 distances proportionally. Public Resources Code § 4291 sets the framework; San Diego County's stricter Zone 1 layers on top of it. Steeper slopes need more clearance — both up-slope and down-slope of the home.

hardening.heading: Home hardening — making the structure ember-resistant
hardening.lead: Most homes lost to wildfire are not ignited by direct flame contact — they are ignited by wind-driven embers that arrive ahead of the fire front and find combustible materials on or near the structure. Hardening closes those entry points. The 2026 California Wildland-Urban Interface Code (CWUIC, Title 24 Part 7), which absorbed the previous Chapter 7A, sets the current performance standards.

roof.heading: Roof
roof.body: Class A fire rating is required in fire hazard severity zones. Acceptable Class A coverings include asphalt fiberglass composition shingles, clay or cement tile, and metal panels. Untreated wood shake is the highest-risk roof type in chaparral country. Keep the roof clear of pine needles, leaves, and other debris. Install a metal drip edge that fully covers the space above the gutter.

vents.heading: Vents
vents.body: Embers entering attic vents is one of the most common ignition paths. Replace any 1/4-inch mesh — too coarse to block embers. Install non-combustible corrosion-resistant metal mesh between 1/16 and 1/8 inch. Avoid fiberglass or plastic mesh — both melt under heat. The upgrade path is California State Fire Marshal-listed ember-resistant WUI vents.

eaves.heading: Eaves and soffits
eaves.body: Enclose with non-combustible or ignition-resistant material. Plug or caulk gaps around rafter tails. On patio covers, box in eaves using the same materials as the roof.

windows.heading: Windows
windows.body: Install dual-paned windows with at least one tempered-glass layer — radiant heat alone can crack single-pane glass before flame contact, allowing embers in. Limit the number and size of windows facing large vegetation areas. Add screens to operable windows.

siding.heading: Siding
siding.body: Stucco, fiber cement, or treated wood are the preferred materials. Extend protective siding from foundation to roofline. Repair gaps, holes, and rot — any opening is an ember entry point.

decks.heading: Decks and patios
decks.body: Use non-combustible or ignition-resistant deck boards. Clear the under-deck area of debris and combustibles. The first 5 ft of any deck adjacent to the house is part of Zone 0 — no combustible furniture, planters, or stored items there.

chimneys.heading: Chimneys
chimneys.body: Install a spark arrestor screen with openings between 3/8 inch and 1/2 inch. Close the fireplace flue during fire season when not in use.

garage.heading: Garage doors
garage.body: Apply weather stripping around and under the door to block embers. Equip the door with a battery backup so it operates during a public safety power shutoff or a grid loss.

fences.heading: Fences
fences.body: Use non-combustible material for the section of fence that connects to the house. Wood fences ignite easily and conduct fire directly to siding and eaves.

rural.heading: Rural-property specifics
rural.lead: Ranchita parcels include features urban defensible-space guides do not cover: outbuildings, propane and fuel tanks, wells and water tanks, long driveways, and solar arrays.

outbuildings.heading: Outbuildings
outbuildings.body: Keep 10 ft of clearance to bare mineral soil around sheds, barns, and workshops, plus an additional 10 ft of no-flammable-vegetation perimeter beyond that. Apply the same hardening principles where feasible — Class A roofs, ember-resistant vents. Outbuildings too close to a home become an ember source if they ignite.

propane.heading: Propane and fuel tanks
propane.body: 10 ft of clearance to bare mineral soil. Keep dry vegetation, woodpiles, and combustibles outside that ring. Mark tanks for fire crews — emergency responders need to identify the location and shut-off valve placement quickly.

water.heading: Wells and water sources
water.body: Mark well housing for fire-crew visibility. If you have a water storage tank, pool, or large trough that crews could draft from in a structure-defense action, install a clear marker visible from the driveway. Keep the approach to any water source clear of debris and obstacles. A 1,500-gallon tank with a hose connection is a significant defense asset.

driveway.heading: Driveways and access
driveway.body: Keep 10 ft of horizontal clearance from each edge of the driveway and 14 ft of overhead clearance for fire engines. Type 3 engines need approximately 10 to 12 ft of width. Gates that swing inward are fastest for crews; better still, have gates that can be removed quickly. Address numbers must be visible from the road, large and reflective.

solar.heading: Solar arrays
solar.body: Keep the array surroundings clear of vegetation. Inverter and battery enclosures benefit from the same hardening — non-combustible siding, ember-resistant vents.

chaparral.heading: Chaparral-specific considerations
chaparral.tip_body: Chaparral is one of the most ember-productive fuel types in California. Ranchita's dominant fuels — chamise, ceanothus, manzanita, and sage — appear on every CAL FIRE high-flammability list.
chaparral.body: Ember storms can travel a mile or more downwind ahead of a fire front. Ember showers ignite structures via vents, accumulated debris, and combustible landscaping long before flame arrives. In drought conditions, fuel moisture stays low year-round — Santa Ana wind events from late summer through winter are the highest-risk periods, but a wind event in any season can produce extreme fire behavior. The 2025 Fire Hazard Severity Zone map update placed virtually the entire San Diego County backcountry in the Very High designation.

calendar.heading: Seasonal maintenance calendar
calendar.lead: Defensible space is not a one-time project. The four-window rhythm below keeps a property in compliance year-round.
calendar.preseason_tasks: Roof inspection. Gutter cleaning. Tree-branch trim — 6 ft above the ground, 10 ft from chimneys. Brush clearance to 50-ft Zone 1. Vent mesh inspection. Propane and outbuilding clearance refresh. Driveway access clearance. Address visibility check.
calendar.season_tasks: Mow annual grass to 4-inch maximum — repeat as it regrows. Daily roof and gutter check during Santa Ana wind events. Test water sources. Confirm Genasys Protect zone code on phones. Confirm trailer is hitched and ready if you have animals.
calendar.postseason_tasks: Post-rain debris removal. Fence repair. Replanting plan — choose fire-resistant species. Equipment maintenance.
calendar.quarterly_tasks: Animal evacuation trailer practice. Water-source signage check. Emergency contact list refresh on phone.

checklist.heading: Printable inspection checklist
checklist.lead: Use this as a quarterly walk-around. The browser print function produces a clean printable version.
checklist.zone1_heading: Zone 1 (5 to 30 ft, or 5 to 50 ft in San Diego County)
checklist.zone2_heading: Zone 2 (30 to 100 ft, or 50 to 100 ft in San Diego County)

animals.title: Animals & Livestock
animals.description: Standalone, comprehensive coverage for pets and livestock — multi-dog households, horses, goats, chickens, evacuation kits, destinations, and shelter-in-place protocols.

intro.tip_body: Multi-dog households are common here — four to six dogs per property is normal — and most parcels have at least some livestock. The closest large-animal evacuation site is roughly 30 miles away in Lakeside. Trailer drives are long. Animal evacuation cannot be improvised. By the time you have the order, the trailer is hitched, the carriers are assembled, and you know exactly which animals go in which vehicle.

principle.heading: Prepare, don't improvise
principle.body: Every animal preparedness step in this guide assumes one principle: the work happens before the warning, not after. A horse that has not been loaded into a trailer in calm conditions cannot be expected to load calmly in smoke. A go-kit assembled during evacuation is an incomplete go-kit. Practice the steps when there is no pressure.

multipet.heading: Multi-pet households (small animals)
multipet.body: Maintain at least one carrier per animal, pre-assembled and labeled with the animal's name, your name, and your phone number. Keep collars on with current ID tags, microchip records up to date, and printed photos of each animal in your go-kit. Have a pre-positioned go-kit (see below). If you cannot move every animal in one trip, decide in advance which animals go first — most vulnerable or most dependent typically lead. If you have multiple drivers in the household, plan multi-vehicle evacuation.

kit.heading: Pet evacuation kit
kit.lead: Per pet, plan for a 7-day supply. The kit lives in a single labeled tote in a known-accessible location during fire season.
kit.food_body: Seven days of dry food (or specialty diet) in a sealed container. Manual can opener if applicable. Two bowls per animal — food and water.
kit.water_body: Approximately one gallon per medium dog per day, half a gallon per cat, scaling up for larger animals. Plan three days minimum, seven preferred.
kit.meds_body: One-week supply of any prescriptions, plus written prescriptions in a waterproof bag so you can refill at a destination pharmacy. Vet's contact info on the outside of the bag.
kit.records_body: Vaccination certificates, microchip numbers, recent photos (refresh annually), owner contact on collar and inside collar pocket, adoption papers or proof of ownership.
kit.comfort_body: One item with home scent (familiar bedding), one favorite toy. Fitted leash or harness, ready to grab.
kit.firstaid_body: Pet first aid kit — gauze, vet wrap, saline rinse, tweezers, styptic powder. Add species-specific items per your veterinarian's guidance.
kit.transport_body: One labeled carrier per animal. Label includes owner name, owner phone, animal name, and any health note.

horses.heading: Horses
horses.body: Practice trailer loading quarterly at minimum. A horse that won't load calmly under stress cannot be expected to load calmly in smoke and confusion. Store halters and leads in a known-accessible location, not scattered through the barn. Plan water at 8 to 12 gallons per horse per day at rest, up to 20 to 25 gallons in hot weather or with heavy work, and 15 to 20 gallons for pregnant or lactating mares. Bring some hay — destinations may run short, especially in the first 24 hours of a major fire. Apply identification you can verify in a chaotic shelter: leg bands, livestock paint marks, or halter tags with your name and phone. Pack a basic hoof care kit and any weather protection your horse normally uses.

small_livestock.heading: Goats, sheep, and llamas
small_livestock.body: Plan water at 2 to 4 gallons per goat or sheep per day, 3 to 5 for llamas. Hot weather increases consumption by 30 to 50 percent. Goats are less docile loaders than horses — practice repeatedly with treats, then short rides, then practice with neighbors' animals so they get used to herd transport. Pasture animals follow each other in motion; lead the dominant animal first.

poultry.heading: Chickens, ducks, and other poultry
poultry.body: Catching during stress is the hardest part. Have a catch net and an enclosed catch area — closing the coop door at night gives you a manageable dawn evacuation. Transport in cardboard pet carriers, cat carriers, or labeled poultry crates: one bird per medium carrier, two or three per large. Plan water at roughly one gallon per eleven birds per day. Poultry overheat fast in trailers — park in shade if you stop, ventilation is critical.

pigs.heading: Pigs
pigs.body: Pigs are the hardest livestock to load under stress. Practice repeatedly with familiar handlers, food rewards, and a short ramp. Plan water at 3 to 5 gallons per finishing pig per day.

shelter_in_place.heading: When you cannot get out — shelter-in-place for animals
shelter_in_place.critical_body: If you cannot evacuate animals safely, your own life takes priority. The protocol below is for animals being left behind, not for any human deciding to stay.
shelter_in_place.body: Open all gates so animals can move away from approaching fire. Remove halters, leads, ropes, and any tie-out — entanglement kills more sheltered animals than smoke does. Move animals to the most defensible cleared area on the property — not a barn. Barns ignite and trap. Provide water in non-combustible containers — metal troughs, not plastic tubs that melt. Mark each animal with livestock paint listing your name and phone. A phone number is what reunifies after the fact. Leave a note on the gate for first responders: animals on property, count, type, owner contact.

destinations.heading: Pre-identified evacuation destinations
destinations.required_body: Activation of these sites is decided by San Diego County Animal Services and CAL FIRE during an active fire. Call 211 or check Genasys Protect / AlertSanDiego for which sites are open during the incident.
destinations.tip_body: Sites that require Animal Care Check Lists or Emergency Evacuation Information packages can lose 15 to 30 minutes per arriving trailer to paperwork that could have been done at the kitchen table. Pre-fill what you can.
destinations.site_djlep_notes: Primary horse evacuation site for east-county fires.
destinations.site_lakeside_notes: Horses and small livestock; activated by SD County Animal Services. Operated by El Capitan Stadium Association (501(c)(3)).
destinations.site_iron_oak_notes: Non-equine large livestock during major county events.
destinations.site_delmar_notes: Largest capacity in the county (approximately 1,600 horses plus other large animals). Activated for major regional events.
destinations.site_sdhs_name: San Diego Humane Society Emergency Response
destinations.site_sdhs_notes: Animal evacuation assistance for both small and large animals. Mutual aid partner of County Animal Services.
destinations.info_line_notes: Current evacuation status, activated shelter sites, and animal services referrals during an active fire.

trailer_practice.heading: Trailer practice calendar
trailer_practice.body: Quarterly minimum. Rotate practice conditions: rain practice, heat practice, late-evening practice (because evacuations often hit at night). Multi-animal practice if you have multiple animals. Practice with the trailer partially loaded — hay, water, feed — so the actual evacuation isn't the first time the trailer is heavy.

insurance.heading: Insurance and identification documentation
insurance.body: Take photos of every animal, including distinguishing markings, color, scars, brands. Refresh annually. Maintain veterinary records — vaccination history, microchip number, Coggins for horses. Record brand inscriptions, microchip numbers, and tattoos. Keep copies in three places: cloud storage so you can access from any device, printed in your home file, printed in the go-kit.

hay_storage.heading: Hay storage safety
hay_storage.body: Hay is one of the highest-fuel-load items on most rural properties. A hay-barn ignition can sustain a structure fire for hours. Store hay separate from livestock barns — if a hay barn ignites, animals shouldn't be in adjacent structures. Maintain at least 100 ft of distance from the main residence, more on slopes. Use ember-resistant covering — metal roof, no exposed soft sides. Don't stack hay against any building wall.

water_dual_use.heading: Water sources that double for firefighting
water_dual_use.body: Pools, large tanks, and stock troughs can serve as draft sources for fire engines. Mark them clearly for crews. Standpipes or hydrants on private property: paint and label visibly. A storage tank with a separate animal supply line is a defense asset and an animal asset simultaneously. Keep the approach to any water source clear and graded for a Type 3 engine.

trigger.heading: When to leave with animals — the trigger
trigger.critical_title: Leave during the watch, not the warning, and never the order
trigger.critical_body: By the time an evacuation order is issued, traffic, smoke, and panic make trailer movement dangerous. Pre-position. Hitch the trailer. Load the kit. Pack the documents. If a Red Flag Warning is issued for your zone in Santa Ana conditions, treat it as the cue to start — do not wait.

checklist.heading: Printable animals pre-fire checklist
checklist.lead: Use this card as a fire-season readiness check. The browser print function produces a clean printable version.

evacuate.title: Evacuate
evacuate.description: The Set, Go, and Return phases — routes, trigger points, alerts and apps, PSPS, smoke and air quality, and re-entry safety.

intro.critical_title: Leave during the watch, not the warning
intro.critical_body: Leave during the watch, not the warning, and never the order. By the time an Evacuation Order is in effect for your zone, traffic, smoke, and panic make every decision harder. The earlier you go, the safer everyone is — including the firefighters who don't have to navigate around evacuating residents.

framework.heading: The Ready–Set–Go framework on the day of a fire
framework.body: The framework that runs through this whole resource has a specific role on an active-fire day. Ready is the work you've already done — defensible space, home hardening, animal preparedness. Set, Go, and Return are the three phases this page covers in detail.

set_phase.heading: Set phase — warning issued
set_phase.body: Set means an Evacuation Warning is in effect for your zone, conditions look like an order is coming, or a Red Flag Warning is active during Santa Ana conditions. Confirm the go-bag is in the vehicle. Confirm the vehicle has at least half a tank. Apply identification to animals — collars, paint marks for livestock, leg bands. Check on neighbors with limited mobility. Hitch the trailer if you have animals. Wear protective clothing: long pants, long-sleeve shirt (cotton is best), heavy shoes, cap, dry bandanna for face cover, eye protection.

go_phase.heading: Go phase — order issued
go_phase.body: Go means an Evacuation Order is in effect for your zone. Leave immediately on the route already in your plan. Don't try to grab last-minute items beyond the kit. Bring neighbors who don't have transportation. Drive with headlights on, inside-air recirculation on, windows up. Don't pass on narrow roads in low visibility. Stay on pavement — fire roads are for firefighters, not evacuees. Once you're moving, stay calm and keep moving.

return_phase.heading: Return phase — re-entry
return_phase.body: Return means authorities have lifted the evacuation order for your zone. Re-entry hazards include charred trees that may fall, weakened power poles, hot debris, downed power lines (assume all are energized), and ash that hides deep heat for days. Wear long pants, long-sleeve shirt, gloves, work boots, and an N95 or P100 respirator while inspecting and cleaning. Document property damage with photographs before disturbing anything. Contact your insurance. Avoid opening interior doors that feel hot. Watch for sparks and embers in mulch, woodpiles, attic spaces, and under decks for at least 72 hours after the fire passes.

routes.heading: Routes from Ranchita
routes.tip_title: Live routing wins over planned routing
routes.tip_body: Specific evacuation routes for an active fire are set by CAL FIRE and the San Diego County Sheriff in real time based on where the fire is. Genasys Protect and AlertSanDiego give the current direction. The framework below is what to understand in advance.
routes.body: Ranchita's primary egress is the S22 corridor (Montezuma Valley Road), with the option of going west toward Julian and Warner Springs or east toward Borrego Springs. Authorities make the call on which direction is open during an active incident — plan to know both. Have a vehicle that can handle either drive on a full tank. If the primary corridor is blocked in both directions and authorities are routing residents through alternates, follow the official routing — do not improvise across unimproved back-routes. Vehicles get stuck on unmaintained roads. Stuck vehicles in evacuation zones become life-threatening situations.

triggers.heading: Trigger points — when to leave early
triggers.required_title: Red Flag plus Santa Ana = readiness state
triggers.required_body: If a Red Flag Warning is in effect for your zone in Santa Ana wind conditions, treat it as the cue to be ready to leave on short notice.
triggers.body: Leave during the watch, not the warning. Reasons to leave early without waiting for an order include any one of: visible smoke from your property in the direction of approaching wind; a Red Flag Warning combined with active fire activity in neighboring zones; loss of cell service or power during a Santa Ana event (information starts going dark before the fire arrives); a household member with mobility limitations or medical needs that take time to move; multiple animals that take time to load. Early evacuation is always the safer choice. Late evacuation risks include traffic, smoke that drops visibility to feet, embers landing on the vehicle, and panic at intersections.

trapped.heading: What to do if you cannot evacuate
trapped.critical_body: If you cannot evacuate, the goal is to survive the fire front passing. The fire front is loud, hot, and brief. Plan to outlast it, not fight it.
trapped.vehicle_body: Park on pavement away from vegetation — outside turn if on a hillside, open lot if available. Turn headlights and emergency flashers on so other vehicles and crews can see you in heavy smoke. Close all windows and doors, shut off air vents, turn off air conditioning. Get below windows, on the floor, under wool blankets if you have them. Call 911 with your location. Stay in the vehicle — do not run. The vehicle is a survival capsule made of glass and steel; running on foot exposes you directly to flame, embers, and radiant heat. Stay until the fire front passes and outside temperatures drop. Then move to an already-burned area where there is no fuel left to burn.
trapped.home_body: Stay inside. Close all exterior doors and windows but leave them unlocked so crews can enter if needed. Move flammable materials — patio furniture, doormats, woodpiles — at least 30 feet from the house. Shut off natural gas and propane. Open gates so animals can move and so fences don't conduct fire to the house. Fill bathtubs, sinks, garbage cans, and pools with water for post-front spot fires. Strategically position sprinklers if water pressure allows. Move to a room with the fewest exterior walls and the smallest windows, away from any direction the fire is approaching from. Stay until the fire front passes. Then inspect the structure, roof, attic, deck, and surrounding ground for embers and small fires for at least the next several hours.
trapped.foot_heading: If on foot in the open
trapped.foot_body: Find an already-burned area. The most dangerous place to be is in unburned fuel. If no burned area is available, find a low spot, lie face-down, cover yourself with anything non-flammable (dirt is fine), protect your airway with a cloth and your eyes with anything available. Wait for the fire front to pass. Embers and radiant heat are the killers; lying low gets you below the worst of both.

apps.heading: Apps and alerts
apps.lead: Install before fire season. A silent app is a useless app — test notifications after install.
apps.genasys_body: Official San Diego County evacuation zone app. Look up your address to learn your zone code. During an evacuation order, the order references zone codes (example format: SDC-1391). Knowing your zone code in advance is how you know whether an order applies to you.
apps.alert_body: Opt-in phone, email, and text alerts from the County. Register at alertsandiego.org. Cell broadcasts reach all phones in an active alert area without registration, but registration captures landlines and email.
apps.sdge_body: Public Safety Power Shutoff (PSPS) and planned-outage notifications. Available as a mobile app and via SMS/email. Backcountry residents are nearly always in PSPS-eligible zones.
apps.watchduty_body: Volunteer-maintained live wildfire tracking, independent of government delays. Useful for situational awareness when an incident is reported but not yet on official county channels.
apps.pulsepoint_body: Real-time fire-department dispatch data. Tells you when units roll on incidents near you.
apps.airnow_body: EPA air quality monitoring. AQI by zip code. Useful before, during, and after fires for smoke exposure decisions.

psps.heading: Public Safety Power Shutoffs (PSPS)
psps.lead: PSPS events are SDG&E proactively turning off power in high-fire-risk weather, primarily Santa Ana wind events in dry vegetation conditions. Backcountry circuits are the most likely to be de-energized.
psps.cadence_body: When conditions allow, SDG&E notifies affected customers in advance: roughly 2 days ahead, 24 hours, 12 hours, 1 to 4 hours, at shutoff, and during restoration. Restoration is not instant — power lines must be patrolled, sometimes by air, before re-energizing. Daylight constraints can extend outage duration overnight.
psps.what_to_do_body: Keep cell phones, laptops, and any medical-baseline devices charged when an event is forecast. Have a battery- or hand-cranked radio for updates. Keep refrigerator and freezer closed — food lasts roughly 4 hours in a fridge and 24 to 48 hours in a full freezer. Generators: know the safety guidelines, never run a generator indoors or in an attached garage, never connect to house wiring without a transfer switch. SDG&E Community Resource Centers open in affected communities with Wi-Fi, phone and medical-device charging, ice, water, snacks, and seasonal supplies — locations published at sdge.com/ready during active events. 211 San Diego provides enhanced PSPS support for medical-baseline customers — accessible transportation, hotel vouchers, back-up power. Free, 24/7, multi-language. Customer service at SDG&E is 800-411-7343. Downed power lines: 911.

smoke.heading: Smoke and air quality
smoke.tip_title: AirNow.gov gives real-time AQI by zip code
smoke.tip_body: Smoke can be dangerous well outside the fire's visible footprint. Check AQI before any decision about going outside.
smoke.body: Wildfire smoke is a mix of fine particulates (PM2.5) and gases. Particulates are the main health threat — they bypass the body's natural defenses and reach deep into the lungs. AQI categories: Good (0 to 50, no special precautions), Moderate (51 to 100, sensitive groups consider limiting outdoor exertion), Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups (101 to 150), Unhealthy (151 to 200), Very Unhealthy (201 to 300), Hazardous (301 and above).
smoke.indoor_body: Keep windows and doors closed. Set HVAC to recirculate, not pull from outside. Run a portable HEPA air cleaner if available. Replace filters with MERV 13 or better where compatible. Designate one room as the cleanest-air room and seal gaps if AQI rises.
smoke.masks_body: NIOSH-approved N95 or P100 respirators give the best protection. KN95 is acceptable. Cloth masks, surgical masks, and bandannas do not filter PM2.5 effectively. Fit matters — a respirator with a poor seal loses most of its benefit. Beards under the seal line break the seal.
smoke.vulnerable_body: Anyone with asthma, COPD, heart disease, pregnancy, young children, and the elderly should be more cautious. Symptoms that warrant medical attention include severe cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, dizziness, and confusion.

reentry.heading: Re-entry safety
reentry.lead_body: Re-entry is when the fire has moved on but the hazards have not. Many fire-related injuries happen in the days after the front passes.
reentry.body: Do not return until authorities lift the evacuation order. The area looks calm but ground heat persists in ash and root systems for days. Charred trees can fall without warning. Weakened power poles and downed lines must be assumed energized. Hot embers in mulch, woodpiles, and attic spaces can flare for 72 hours or more.
reentry.ppe_body: Long pants, long-sleeve shirt, gloves, work boots, N95 or P100 respirator. Document damage with photographs before moving anything. Contact insurance. Inspect the roof, attic, decks, fences, propane fittings. Check water lines for damage. If gas service was on during the fire, check with the utility before relighting pilots — don't light a match, candle, or cigarette anywhere on the property until you've cleared this with the gas utility.

checklist.heading: Printable evacuate-phase checklist
checklist.lead: The browser print function produces a clean printable version of the checklist below.

mutual_aid.title: Mutual Aid
mutual_aid.description: How we show up for each other in Ranchita — and stay out of the way of the people fighting fires. Year-round community work, neighbor check-in lists, communications, and the line between help and interference.

intro.para_1: Ranchita's geography means professional response is volunteer-supported and farther away than in town. The closest crews are minutes — sometimes longer — from any address in the valley, and the people first on a scene are almost always neighbors.
intro.para_2: The way we look out for each other here isn't a workaround. It's part of how this place actually functions, year-round, not just during fires. The work that follows on this page is what neighbor-helping-neighbor looks like in practice — before, during, and after.

support.heading: How residents support firefighters
support.critical_body: Show up for your neighbors. Stay out of the way of the firefighters. The line between those two is the discipline this section is about.
support.body: Residents are first on most fire scenes by definition of geography. Useful resident actions during an incident include wetting down your own property, wetting down a neighbor's property if you can do it safely, moving animals, checking on elderly neighbors, clearing your driveway for engine access. Useful resident actions that do NOT happen near the fire line include anything that puts you in the same airspace, water source, or road segment as professional crews. Crews work to a plan that you don't have visibility into. Standing in their water source, blocking their road segment, or asking them questions during an active push wastes time they don't have.

year_round.heading: Year-round community work
year_round.lead: The work that matters most happens months before any fire.
year_round.roads_body: The community-scale extension of the Zone 3 access principle. Branches that hang into S22 or feeder roads, brush that encroaches on the shoulders, gate-side vegetation that blocks engine access — these are the things neighbors clearing together on a Saturday morning in spring address before fire season. Coordinate via existing community channels.
year_round.chipping_body: Defensible-space work generates piles of brush, branches, and slash. A community chipping day rents a chipper for a weekend, neighbors rotate through, and the cleared material gets reduced to mulch (which, ironically, gets used in Zone 2, not Zone 0). Spring is the right window. Cost-share by parcel.
year_round.water_body: The community knows where the water is — pools, large tanks, stock troughs that fire crews could draft from. Compile that map. Update it annually as properties change. Share it with the local fire authority through proper channels — the local San Diego County Fire station, not a one-off conversation with an engine crew during a fire. Marked water sources visible from the driveway help crews who don't know your property.
year_round.driveway_body: A driveway that can't accept a Type 3 engine isn't useful in a fire — it's a liability. The community can help each other with gate hinge orientation, overhead clearance trims, address-number reflective signage, and turnaround space at the back of long driveways. The work of one weekend on a property whose owner can't physically do it themselves is the most concrete form of mutual aid available.

checkin.heading: Neighbor check-in lists
checkin.lead_body: The people most at risk in an evacuation are the ones who need help and don't ask. The community list is how that gets addressed.
checkin.body: Identify, with permission and respectfully, the neighbors who would benefit from a check-in if a Red Flag Warning hits. Categories that often need help include elderly residents living alone, residents with mobility limitations, residents on home medical equipment dependent on power, single-parent households with small children, households with many animals to load, residents without transportation, and residents who travel for work and may be away when an order hits. The list lives somewhere multiple neighbors can access — not on one person's phone. Update at least annually. Privacy matters: this list contains sensitive personal information and should be treated accordingly.

comms.heading: Communications during incidents
comms.tip_title: Cell service is not reliable here in Santa Anas
comms.tip_body: Cell service in Ranchita is not reliable during Santa Ana wind events. Plan for the day cell goes down.
comms.lead: Redundancy is the answer. Cell phones first, but assume they will be unreliable.
comms.phone_tree_body: A predetermined order of who calls whom. Five neighbors in a tree, each calling one other neighbor and so on, gets information moving without overloading any one person. Update when residents move in or out.
comms.frs_body: Family Radio Service handhelds (no license required) and General Mobile Radio Service (license required for transmit, one license covers a household) give a backup that works when cell does not. A common channel agreed in advance lets neighbors check in across the valley without infrastructure. Range varies with terrain — line-of-sight is best.
comms.ham_body: Amateur radio is the most resilient option for genuine grid-down conditions. Licensing takes a weekend course and an exam. For communities with one or two licensed operators, the rest of the community gets reach beyond what FRS or GMRS provides. Local clubs can advise.
comms.rally_body: A specific physical location everyone in the community knows as the place to check in if comms are down. Outside the immediate fire path, on the egress route, with parking. This is not the evacuation site itself — it's the local confirmation-of-status point before people move on.

line.heading: When help is help — and when it's interference
line.never_body: Never insert yourself between firefighters and the fire. Never use a water source crews are drafting from. Never enter an evacuated zone to retrieve property.
line.body: The difference between mutual aid and getting in the way is mostly about staying in your own role. Your role is your property, your neighbors' property at their request, and the people in your community who need help moving. The fire line is not your role. Residents who try to defend against an active fire front directly, without training and equipment, become casualties — and become a search-and-rescue burden that pulls firefighters off the actual fire. The most useful thing a resident can do during the active push is to have already evacuated, with their neighbors, with their animals, on the route the authorities are routing to. The most useful thing during pre-fire conditions is the year-round work above.

after.heading: After the fire — rebuilding together
after.lead: The recovery is longer than the fire. The community's work is most needed in the weeks and months after.
after.body: Neighbors help each other navigate insurance claims, document photographs, debris removal, and rebuilding permits. The county opens recovery resources after major events; a community that coordinates the application of those resources gets more benefit than households navigating individually.
after.emotional_body: Post-fire is a long mental-health tail. Residents who lost homes, who lost animals, who watched the fire come close, who evacuated multiple times in one season — these are the people who need community presence in the months after the smoke clears. The check-in list stays active well past the all-clear.

checklist.heading: Printable mutual-aid checklist
checklist.lead: The browser print function produces a clean printable version of the checklist below.

neighbor_ethos.heading: Neighbors helping neighbors
neighbor_ethos.body: In Ranchita, help is far away. The closest professional firefighters are minutes — sometimes longer — from any address in the valley, and the people first on a scene are almost always neighbors. That's not a failing of the system; it's the geography. The way we look out for each other here is part of the system. We wet down a neighbor's roof when they're at work two hours away. We move livestock when the smoke shifts. We check on the elderly couple at the end of the road before we leave for our own evacuation. And — just as importantly — we know when to step back, so the firefighters and the trained crews who arrive can do their job without us in the way. Showing up for each other doesn't mean playing fire department. It means being ready, being aware, and knowing the line.

risk.heading: Local risk profile
risk.topography_para: S22 places Ranchita at a ridgeline where prevailing winds and terrain can drive fire downhill toward Borrego Springs in one direction and through canyons toward the western mountain communities in the other. The single S22 corridor is the primary egress for most addresses; alternates exist but are slower and require local knowledge.
risk.fuels_para: Dominant fuels are mature chaparral (manzanita, ceanothus, chamise) with pockets of oak and pine. Chaparral is one of the most ember-productive fuel types in California — embers can travel a mile or more downwind ahead of a fire front, igniting structures via vents, debris, and combustible landscaping long before the flame arrives.
risk.winds_para: Santa Ana wind events from late summer through winter are the highest-risk periods, but drought-year fuel moisture means a wind event in any season can produce extreme fire behavior. Plan year-round.
risk.response_para: Backcountry response is volunteer-supported. Mutual aid arrives, but with travel time. The first thirty minutes of any fire belong to whoever is on scene — usually you and your neighbors.

contacts.heading: Emergency contacts
contacts.subhead: Save these to your phone now. Cell service is unreliable during fires.
contacts.info_note: Current evacuation status, shelter availability, animal services referrals.
contacts.genasys_note: Official SD County evacuation zone app. Look up your zone before fire season.
contacts.alert_note: Opt-in phone, email, and text alerts from the County.

not_replacement.title: This guide does not replace official guidance
not_replacement.body: Read CAL FIRE's Ready for Wildfire program and San Diego County Fire's Ready, Set, Go! Wildland Fire Action Guide directly. This page extends them with Ranchita-specific layers — it does not substitute for either.

draft_notice.title: Active draft
draft_notice.body: This resource is in active draft. MVVCSO is refining content with the local board. Some sections will continue to evolve as board members and longtime community members layer in their firsthand experience.

phases.ready_summary: Defensible space, home hardening, animal preparedness — done before there is a fire.
phases.set_summary: Watch is issued. Pre-position. Stay alert.
phases.go_summary: Evacuation order. Leave on the route you've already chosen.
phases.return_summary: Re-entry hazards: power lines, weakened trees, hot debris.
